title: Pick three
summary: A long-serving pool manager gives a new lifeguard exactly three rules. She also tells him how to find the third person to watch.

body.speaker: Karin
body.text: I am not Mrs Karlsen. I am Karin.

body.speaker: Karin
body.text: Better. The pool is yours from nine to one.

body.speaker: Karin
body.text: I have managed the locker rooms for thirty-two years. I know the swimmers better than I know my own daughter. I will tell you the three most important things.

body.speaker: Karin
body.text: One. The old man with the blue cap, Olav. He swims sixty laps every morning. He has had a heart problem since 2017. Watch him on lap fifty. If he stops at the wall for more than thirty seconds, jump in.

body.speaker: Karin
body.text: Two. The young girl with the pink swimsuit, Astrid. She is nine. She is afraid of the deep end. Her father wants her to swim across. Do not encourage her. Let her decide. If she says 'I am ready,' swim beside her. Not behind. Beside.

body.speaker: Karin
body.text: Three. The pool is busiest at eleven. Thirty swimmers. You cannot watch all of them. Pick three. Watch those three with your full eyes. The others know how to swim. Three is the number a lifeguard can hold in his head.

body.speaker: Tomas
body.text: Three. Olav, Astrid, and someone else.

body.speaker: Karin
body.text: Not someone else. Whoever looks tired. Tired changes every day.

body.speaker: Tomas
body.text: Karin, may I ask one question?

body.speaker: Tomas
body.text: How will I know who looks tired?

body.speaker: Karin
body.text: You will know in two weeks. Until then, watch everybody. After two weeks, you will see only the ones who matter. Now. Take this whistle. It is yours.

annotations.meaningVi: lượt bơi (từ đầu này sang đầu kia bể)
phòng thay đồ (ở trung tâm thể thao, bể bơi)
vấn đề về tim / bệnh tim
nhảy xuống (nước) — cụm động từ chỉ hành động cứu hộ khẩn cấp
bơi sang (từ bờ này qua bờ kia)
để cô ấy tự quyết định — cấu trúc 'let + tân ngữ + động từ nguyên mẫu'
giữ trong đầu / nhớ được — thành ngữ chỉ năng lực ghi nhớ đồng thời
đông đúc / bận rộn nhất — dạng so sánh nhất của 'busy'
những người thực sự quan trọng / đáng chú ý
tôi có thể hỏi không — mẫu câu lịch sự dùng 'may' để xin phép
quan sát tất cả mọi người — 'watch' nhấn mạnh sự chú ý liên tục, khác với 'see'

grammarNotes.title: 'Will' for predictions about a learner's future
grammarNotes.bodyVi: A2 dùng 'will + động từ' để dự đoán điều người học sẽ trải qua. Trong bài, Karin dùng nhiều câu 'will' để vẽ ra đường học việc của Tomas: 'You will know in two weeks.' 'After two weeks, you will see only the ones who matter.' 'I will tell you the three most important things.' 'There will be thirty swimmers.' Đây là 'will' của dự đoán có cơ sở kinh nghiệm, không phải quyết định ngay lúc nói.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: A2 uses 'will + verb' to predict what a learner will experience. Karin sketches Tomas's training arc with several 'will' sentences: 'You will know in two weeks.' 'After two weeks, you will see only the ones who matter.' 'I will tell you the three most important things.' This is 'will' of experience-based prediction, not a decision made at the moment of speaking.

translationVi:
Karin: Tomas. Ngày đầu. Ngồi.
Tomas: Vâng, bà Karlsen.
Karin: Không phải 'bà Karlsen'. Là Karin.
Tomas: Vâng, Karin.
Karin: Tốt hơn. Bể bơi của cậu từ chín giờ đến một giờ.
Tomas: Bể của tôi?
Karin: Tôi quản lý phòng thay đồ ba mươi hai năm. Tôi biết khách bơi rõ hơn cả con gái mình. Tôi sẽ nói cậu ba điều quan trọng nhất.
Tomas: Tôi nghe.
Karin: Một. Ông cụ đội mũ xanh, Olav. Ông bơi sáu mươi lượt mỗi sáng. Ông có vấn đề tim từ 2017. Để mắt ông ở lượt năm mươi. Nếu ông dừng ở mép bể quá ba mươi giây, nhảy xuống.
Tomas: Hiểu.
Karin: Hai. Cô bé mặc đồ bơi hồng, Astrid. Chín tuổi. Sợ đầu sâu. Cha cháu muốn cháu bơi sang. Đừng khích lệ cháu. Để cháu tự quyết. Nếu cháu nói 'cháu sẵn sàng', bơi bên cạnh cháu. Không phải đằng sau. Bên cạnh.
Tomas: Bên cạnh cô bé.
Karin: Ba. Bể đông nhất lúc mười một. Ba mươi người bơi. Cậu không thể nhìn hết. Chọn ba người. Nhìn ba người đó bằng toàn bộ mắt. Người khác biết bơi. Ba là số một cứu hộ giữ được trong đầu.
Tomas: Ba. Olav, Astrid, và một người khác.
Karin: Không phải 'người khác'. Ai trông mệt. 'Mệt' thay đổi mỗi ngày.
Tomas: Karin, tôi hỏi một câu được không?
Karin: Được.
Tomas: Làm sao tôi biết ai trông mệt?
Karin: Hai tuần nữa cậu sẽ biết. Đến đó, nhìn mọi người. Sau hai tuần, cậu sẽ chỉ thấy người quan trọng. Giờ. Cầm cái còi này. Của cậu.

questions.prompt: How long has Karin managed the locker rooms?
questions.explanation: 'I have managed the locker rooms for thirty-two years.'

options: The old man with the blue cap; swims sixty laps a morning; has had a heart problem since 2017.
questions.explanation: Karin describes him in rule one.

questions.prompt: When should Tomas jump in for Olav?
options: If he stops at the wall for more than thirty seconds, especially on lap fifty.
questions.explanation: 'If he stops at the wall for more than thirty seconds, jump in.'

options: A nine-year-old with a pink swimsuit; afraid of the deep end; her father wants her to swim across.
questions.explanation: Karin describes her in rule two.

questions.prompt: What should Tomas NOT do about Astrid?
options: Encourage her — let her decide; if she says 'I am ready,' swim beside her, not behind.
questions.explanation: 'Do not encourage her. Let her decide.'

questions.prompt: When is the pool busiest, and how many swimmers come?
questions.explanation: 'The pool is busiest at eleven. Thirty swimmers.'

questions.prompt: How many swimmers should Tomas watch with full attention?
questions.explanation: 'Pick three. Watch those three with your full eyes. Three is the number a lifeguard can hold in his head.'

options: Whoever looks tired — and 'tired changes every day.'
questions.explanation: 'Whoever looks tired. Tired changes every day.'

questions.prompt: How will Tomas learn to see 'tired'?
options: By watching everybody for two weeks; then he will see only the ones who matter.
questions.explanation: 'You will know in two weeks. Until then, watch everybody. After two weeks, you will see only the ones who matter.'

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: A skill built on attention starts with watching everyone and gradually narrows to the three people who matter that day.
Lifeguards should refuse all but three swimmers.
Pools should be closed at eleven.
questions.explanation: Karin teaches a method of attention, not a fixed list.
questions.hint: Notice that the 'three' includes one changing person.

cloze.template:
Karin: Tomas. First day. Sit.
Tomas: Yes, Mrs Karlsen.
Karin: I am not Mrs Karlsen. I am Karin.
Tomas: Yes, Karin.
Karin: Better. The pool is yours from nine to {{b1}}.
Tomas: The pool is mine?
Karin: I have managed the locker rooms for thirty-{{b2}} years. I know the swimmers better than I know my own daughter. I will tell you the three most important things.
Tomas: I'm listening.
Karin: One. The old man with the blue cap, Olav. He swims sixty {{b3}} every morning. He has had a heart problem since 2017. Watch him on lap fifty. If he stops at the wall for more than thirty seconds, {{b4}} in.
Tomas: Got it.
Karin: Two. The young girl with the pink swimsuit, Astrid. She is nine. She is afraid of the {{b5}} end. Her father wants her to swim across. Do not encourage her. Let her decide. If she says 'I am ready,' swim {{b6}} her. Not behind. Beside.
Tomas: Beside her.
Karin: Three. The pool is busiest at {{b7}}. Thirty swimmers. You cannot watch all of them. Pick three. Watch those three with your full eyes. The others know how to swim. Three is the number a lifeguard can hold in his head.
Tomas: Three. Olav, Astrid, and someone else.
Karin: Not someone else. Whoever looks {{b8}}. Tired changes every day.
Tomas: Karin, may I ask one question?
Karin: Yes.
Tomas: How will I know who looks tired?
Karin: You {{b9}} know in two weeks. Until then, watch everybody. After two weeks, you will see only the ones who matter. Now. Take this {{b10}}. It is yours.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Karin tells Tomas the third person to watch is 'whoever looks tired' that day — not a fixed name. Is there a place in your work or family where you have been keeping the same three names on your watch-list, when the real third 'tired' person changes weekly? How would you change what you do if you accepted that?
